pathophysiology_summary: Herpes labialis is reactivation of latent HSV-1 in the trigeminal ganglion, triggered by stress, illness, UV or fatigue. It presents with a prodromal tingle/burn followed by grouped vesicles on an erythematous base at the vermilion border, which rupture, crust and heal in 7–10 days. It is a clinical diagnosis readily made on video and is benign and self-limiting; oral antivirals shorten the episode when started in the prodrome/first 48–72h. The ground-truth tasks are to (1) recognise it and offer early antiviral, (2) screen for the sight-threatening complication of ocular HSV (herpetic keratitis — red/painful/photophobic eye) and prevent autoinoculation, and (3) exclude the dangerous mimics: eczema herpeticum (widespread punched-out lesions on eczema — needs urgent systemic antiviral), facial erysipelas/cellulitis (spreading hot red swelling), and Stevens-Johnson syndrome (mucosal sloughing/target lesions, systemic toxicity).

differential.evidence_basis: Prodromal tingle then grouped vesicles at the vermilion border with a recurrent history.
Honey-crusting and spread beyond the lip would suggest impetigo/secondary infection — not the primary pattern here.
Screen for widespread punched-out lesions on eczematous skin with systemic features — needs urgent systemic antiviral.
Autoinoculation to the eye is sight-threatening — screen ocular symptoms.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Cold sore leads; ocular HSV and eczema herpeticum/erysipelas/SJS must be excluded before routine reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: No ocular involvement, no widespread/eczematous lesions, no spreading cellulitis or mucosal/systemic features — uncomplicated recurrent cold sore.
Eyes asymptomatic; escalation target if ocular symptoms develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative ocular/systemic screen, uncomplicated herpes labialis leads; early antiviral + infection control.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting; antiviral shortens it if early; recurrence expected.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign recurrent cold sore.

red_flags.description: Ocular HSV — red, painful or photophobic eye, or peri-ocular vesicles
red_flags.clinical_significance: Herpetic keratitis can scar the cornea and threaten sight — needs urgent ophthalmology, not just oral antiviral.

red_flags.description: Eczema herpeticum / facial erysipelas-cellulitis / Stevens-Johnson syndrome — widespread lesions, spreading hot red swelling, mucosal sloughing/target lesions, systemic toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: These are dermatological emergencies needing urgent systemic treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening the eye — autoinoculation herpetic keratitis is sight-threatening.
Overlooking widespread punched-out lesions in an atopic patient needing urgent systemic antiviral.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fluorescein slit-lamp examination for corneal dendritic ulcer
Full skin examination for disseminated/eczematous involvement
Viral PCR swab (not needed for a classic case)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Offer early oral antiviral + infection-control advice; urgent eye care for any ocular symptoms; ED for eczema herpeticum/erysipelas/SJS features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing ocular HSV or eczema herpeticum behind a 'cold sore'.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recurrent herpes labialis: oral antiviral (e.g. valaciclovir/famciclovir/aciclovir) is most effective started in the prodrome or within 48–72h; topical measures for comfort; infection-control advice. Urgent review for ocular involvement or immunocompromise.
